## Overview

[Sergio Correia's HDFE benchmark suite](https://www.scorreia.com/data/hdfe/index.html)
consists of a collection of real-world datasets that stress different graph topologies and are the most extensive collection of public benchmark data sets for HDFE solvers we are aware of.

We benchmark PyFixest against the following references:

- [**fixest**](https://lrberge.github.io/fixest/), which is the reference HDFE implementation in R. `fixest` implements a MAP-style solver with Irons-Tuck acceleration and other optimizations.
- [**FixedEffectModels.jl**](https://github.com/FixedEffects/FixedEffectModels.jl), which is the reference Julia implementation and implements an LSMR solver with diagonal preconditioning.

All benchmarks are run on Alex' local machine (Mac mini, Apple M4, 10 cores, 16 GB RAM, macOS 15.3.1).

We fit two-way fixed effects regressions using each package's defaults: multicollinear variables are dropped (using package-specific algorithms), singletons are optionally dropped, and iid inference is used. We run each benchmark with and without singleton dropping. We use a tolerance of `1e-8`. Note that convergence criteria differ across implementations, so the tolerance is only a common stopping threshold and does not guarantee identical convergence behavior.

::: {.callout-note}
Benchmarks for [**reghdfe**](https://scorreia.com/software/reghdfe/) (Stata) can be found on [Sergio Correia's website](https://www.scorreia.com/data/hdfe/index.html). Note, however, that those numbers were collected roughly nine years ago on older hardware, so Python, R, and Julia implementations have a substantial hardware advantage in any direct comparison.
:::

::: {.callout}
For an explanation why different fixed effects / graph structures lead to differences in solver performance, please take a look at the [When Are Fixed Effects Estimations Difficult?](difficult-fixed-effects.md) vignette.
:::

## Reproducing these numbers

To run the benchmarks yourself, you can use the `pixi` tasks below, which will automatically install all required (open source) dependencies, create synethetic data sets and run the benchmarks. You will have to download the Correia datasets from [Correia's website](https://www.scorreia.com/data/hdfe/index.html) and place them in the `benchmarks/modular/data/correia` directory.

```bash
# benchmark fixest-dgp's
pixi run benchmark
# benchmark R, Julia, Pyton on the Correia datasets
pixi run benchmark-correia
```
